What should we do? Yeah? So we can turn all the Latin vowels into A. Yeah, so if we do it the other way around, if we say the original vowels were more like the Latin vowels, and Sanskrit underwent a bunch of sound changes that squashed many of the vowels together. So the original vowels, E and O, and also A, like the first vowel in field, all three of those vowels became A, A in Sanskrit.

So this is the same kind of reasoning we were using when we were originally doing phonology. We can't predict the Latin vowels from the Sanskrit vowels, but we can predict the Sanskrit vowels from the Latin vowels. Namely, the Latin vowels all got squashed together. So Latin has kept an older vowel system that Sanskrit has simplified to a certain extent. So we'll pause at a Proto-Indo-European stem, E for E, Sanskrit undergoes a change where the E becomes A, English undergoes Grimm's Law, and also some changes with the vowels, which we'll get a chance to talk about in a second.

And Latin sort of preserves at least that much of the Proto-Indo-European form. It's worth being careful. Just like at the beginning of this class when we were doing phonology, we started off with sound changes, looking at data sets, where it was often the case that you could posit an underlying form that was the same as some form that you could see on the surface. But I think we saw back when we were doing phonology that that wasn't necessarily true.

There were cases where it was useful to posit an underlying form that always underwent some kind of change, you know, that never just surfaced in its actual form. And this also happens when we're positing Proto-Forms. It hasn't happened in this particular case. So I carefully picked a case where life was simple and Latin has just sort of kept the Proto-Indo-European form. But we can't rely on that happening every time.

Sometimes there are cases where we'll want to posit a Proto-Form that just doesn't survive in its unchanged form in any case. Sound changes, you know, the study of sound changes, first of all, the discovery that sound changes were regular, that you could make statements of the form wherever this language has sound X, this other language has sound Y. That was a big early discovery in linguistics and sort of the starting point for a lot of the work that started getting done in phonology as people started getting interested in the questions of what kinds of sound changes there were and why some kinds of sound changes were more common than others and.
You know, what motivated them and why they happened and so on.

Similarly, the discussion that we had when we were doing phonology of rule ordering where we decided that it was useful sometimes to posit, we talked about this for Lardal, for example, that it's useful sometimes to posit an original form, an underlying form, and have it undergo sound changes in a particular order, right? I think we talked about it as being like an assembly line, right? It first goes to the place where it undergoes this sound change and then it undergoes this sound change with the consequence sometimes that the second sound change creates something that would undergo the first sound change, but it's too late, we looked at examples like that.

So, you know, way back when, when we were talking about Lardal, we had a rule that changed u to a at the ends of words that changed underlying mugu, which is the underlying word for water, to muga. And we had another sound change that got rid of k at the ends of words. So if you had a k at the end of the word, it would drop, which changed underlying ngaluk, which is the word for story, to ngalu. Okay.

But it was important that these rules applied in this order because ngalu doesn't then change to ngala. That was one of the kinds of cases we were talking about when we were talking about ordering rules in a particular order. All of that talk started in work on historical linguistics where it's easy to imagine, in fact, that a bunch of sound changes have taken place, and they've taken place in a particular order.

People got interested in possible relations between sound changes. Some kinds of sound changes are cross-linguistically fairly common. So here's one, w changed to gu, for example, in Chamorro. Chamorro is a language of Saipan in the Pacific. It's closely related to Tagalog, which is a language of the Philippines. They're both Austronesian languages. So for example, Tagalog has a word asawa, which means spouse. So Tagalog kinship terminology mostly doesn't indicate the gender of the person you're talking about.

So they don't have words for husband or wife. They just have this word asawa, which means spouse. You can say, you know, male spouse or female spouse, but you don't, usually. So Tagalog has a word asawa. In Chamorro, this is a sagwa. So the w has become a gu. Tagalog has a number, dalawa, which is the number two, that corresponds to hugwa in Chamorro. And this wala, which we were talking about before, which means there isn't, just in Tagalog, all over Austronesian means there is, and that's what it means in Chamorro.

But it doesn't start with a w in Chamorro, it starts with a gu. If you think about what a w is, the fact that a w becomes a gu in Chamorro is maybe not such a weird fact. So think about what a w is. A w is a glide that corresponds to the vowel oo. If you think back, way, way back, months ago when you were young and carefree and knew a lot about phonology and had not yet encountered, you know, syntax and semantics.

Maybe you remember that the vowel oo has rounding, right? Your lips are rounding. But it's also high and back, right? What that means is that your tongue is bunched up toward the top of the back of your mouth. So back then we were doing group exercises like saying ooey over and over again. And when you did that, you discovered that your lips were rounding and unrounding, but also that your tongue was shifting from the back of your mouth to the front of your mouth.

So w is a glide that corresponds to the vowel oo. It's a version of the vowel oo said very fast, basically. In which your tongue is bunching up toward the back of your mouth, reaching toward the top of the back of your mouth, and your lips are rounding. So if you think about what you do when you do a g, a g, a voiced velar stop, well, what you're doing is your tongue is touching the back of your mouth, right.

It's making a velar closure back there, yeah? So the fact that a w has become a gu in this language is sort of understandable. A gu is just a w that got out of hand, right? It's your tongue not just bunching up toward the back of your mouth, but actually colliding with it, right? Making a closure back there, yeah. And I'm going through all this because this sound change didn't just happen in Chamorro.

It also happened in Welsh. So Welsh has a bunch of words. I've just given you one, which is man. The Welsh word for man is guir. It's related to the Latin word vir, which we have as virile in English, words like that. The vir is actually also, I think, related to the weir in weir wolf, which is a man, a man-wolf, yeah. Welsh has another word which they borrowed from, borrowed from Latin, guin, which is wine.

So they borrowed that from Latin. They changed the w sound at the beginning to a gu. So Latin underwent, Welsh, sorry, underwent the same sound change. There are also some words that got borrowed from Proto-Germanic into late Latin. That is Latin right before it was about to break up and turn into all the Romance languages, in which the Proto-Germanic w got realized in late Latin as a gu.

So the Proto-Germanic word for war, which is guera, ancestor of the modern English war, got borrowed into late Latin as guera. And you now see that in English words like guerrilla, right, in the sense of a person who fights a particular type of warfare. That, if you study any Romance languages, the old Latin word for war was bellum, I think. But that word, I believe, vanished from all the Romance languages.

What they all have are descendants of this word. So, you know, if you study Italian or French or Spanish, you need to learn words for war like guerre and guera. Those are all descendants of this Germanic word. So the late Latins, I guess, were really impressed with the Germanic attitude toward war. They borrowed this word and they completely replaced their word for war with it, as far as I can tell. Do we know war bellum was again? No.

At least I don't. Yeah, this just became the cool word for war. So I mean, we have it in expressions that we've borrowed from Latin like antebellum, right? But as far as their normal word for war, it got replaced with this. We also have words in English where we have both of these words. So there's the Proto-Germanic word for guard, which was ward. We have that as a word, ward, right, to ward off a threat or something like that.

It got borrowed into late Latin as guard. That's sort of the modern word for watch in lots of languages. So in Italian, if you want to watch TV, the verb you use is guardare. You guard the TV. You watch it carefully. And English has both of these words now. So we have ward and we also have guard. They both have the same root. They're both from Proto-Germanic ward, which came to us straight in the form of ward and then took a little detour into Latin to become guard and then we borrowed it again in a different form.

So, big discovery then, you know, Grimm's law, this change from W to Gua, it's not, so you know, lots of big discoveries. But one of the big discoveries was that sound change was regular. You could do better than just saying these words look kind of similar to each other and that these languages are related. You could actually say, oh yeah, these words are, these languages are related because there is this general law about how sounds in language X are related to sound in language Y.

This one's called the Neo-Grammarian Hypothesis and it was one of the big early discoveries. It shifts emphasis away from looking at lists of words that look kind of similar. What we're looking for are lists of words that can be related by regular sound change laws, which is not the same thing. There could be lots of sound changes. The sound changes could be really radical. The result could be that the words don't look like each other at all.

The point is to discover that there are regular correspondences between the words in the lists. Does that make sense? That's how you discover that two languages are related. So just to give you a dramatic example of this, here are three words for the number two in three languages whose names I have cunningly hidden from you. We'll call them language A, language B, and language C. And I will tell you that two of these words are related to each other and the third is not.

You're probably suspecting a trick, but go ahead. Somebody fall for it. Which of these two languages do you think are related to each other? Yes? B and C. Yes, C. You're right. But that's because you know how this kind of thing works. You're supposed to say A and B. Would somebody go ahead and say A and B? I would feel better. Is it A and B? Yes. Yes.

It's A and B. Excellent suggestion. Thank you. But of course, he's right. It is B and C. Er is the Mandarin word for two. Erku is the Armenian word for two, and Greek has duo as its word for two. The way you convince yourself of this is by discovering that wherever Greek has a duo, Armenian has an erk, which is a pretty radical sound change. It took people a while to discover that Armenian was an Indo-European language, because Armenian has had really radical things happen to it, with the consequence that its words don't look all that Indo-European anymore.

But it underwent this general sound change, and the point is you discover that the sound change is general, and that's how you convince yourself that it belongs to this club. Yeah? Why? Why did it do that? So there isn't any really good answer to that, but you could think. If you imagined first that they decided that it wasn't good to have words start with D and W, like that's too many consonants at the beginning, we don't want that.

We're going to add a vowel, so they'll add a vowel at the beginning. So this alternation between D and R is not such a strange thing to do. So an R is kind of, it's not unlike the relationship between W and U, you know, that D is an actual stop, and R, it is unlike that. That D is an actual stop, and R, especially if it's a flap, is you're just sort of whacking the part of your mouth where you make the D stop.

And then the fact that W became a K, well, we've seen W becoming a W, right, in Welsh and in Chamorro, and you know, a K has a stop in a place where a W would just be an approximate. So it's maybe not a completely crazy set of sound changes. I should say, you know, it's not always possible to understand why a language underwent the sound changes that it did.

There's a beautiful example, I have sometimes used this as a problem set in this class. If you were going to have another problem set, it might have been about this. There's an Algonquian language, Arapaho. If you look at the Algonquian languages, the Proto-Algonquian can be reconstructed to a fair degree of confidence. There are a bunch of Algonquian languages, they're very well studied. There are sound changes, it's not always hard to relate them to each other.

Arapaho is an Algonquian language, but it has undergone sound changes like, I'm trying to remember them all, P has become ch, M has become, what has M become? M has become B. There's a sound. So with the consequence that, oh no, M has become ch, they've both become ch, they've blended together. So the consequence is that, you know, the word for dog, which is the one that I can remember off the top of my head, there's a word for dog that in Wampanoag, which is spoken around here, is anum, this is a schwa.

And in Passamaquoddy is alum, and in Cree is atim. And so, you know, there's an M here that corresponds, and there are vowels here that have undergone various kinds of sound changes. There's a sound here, which is kind of mysterious, it's unclear how to represent it in Proto-Algonquian. But, so, you know, here's Wampanoag, here's Passamaquoddy, here's Cree, and the Arapaho word for it is, Fanch.

Yeah. So you've got, anum, alum, atim, and then Fanch is the Arapaho word. So the Arapaho's, I don't know what happened to them, but, they, they underwent sound changes that, it's very hard to, to justify to yourself. It's unclear, unclear what happened. I, was once talking with a linguist about this, and his theory was that, what happened with the Arapaho was that they, they, they became a horse culture.

When horses were brought to the U.S., the Arapaho got really into horses, and, his theory was there must have been a generation of Arapaho who just sort of stopped listening to their parents, you know, and underwent all kinds of radical sound changes because they were, like, they were too busy riding horses. So, so, you know, this is an example where, thank you, one of you, deliberately fell for the trap that I was setting, an example where you convince yourself that the, these two, the Mandarin and the Armenian words, you know, are not related despite having some stuff that sort of looks like it's in.
Common, it's really the Armenian.

And the Greek words, that are related. Another example where you have two words that look similar but, the two languages are not related. There's a beautiful, memoir by a guy named, Dixon, Robert Dixon is a, linguist who did, he wrote a, a book called Memoirs of a Field Worker, which was, about his work in aboriginal languages in Australia in the sixties, it's a nice book.

And one of the things he talks about is, going, he was traveling around different, aboriginal, areas, different, talking to different, speakers of different languages. And, there was a particular language that he managed to find possibly one of the last speakers of, language called Mbābaram, which no longer has any speakers at all. This was in the sixties. And, he was very interested in this language.

It was supposed to be quite different from some of the languages that were near it. And he finally found a speaker who was finally willing to talk to him and sat down with this guy and the guy said, okay, let me start teaching you some Mbābaran words. Do you know our word for dog? And he was like, okay, no, what's your word for dog? And the guy smiled and he said, dog.

Yeah. And, Dixon was like, okay, this has to be a joke, but it turned out it was not a joke. Most of the languages of the world probably have a word for dog. There are dogs in lots of places. And, you know, D and O and G, they're not such uncommon sounds and, 6,000, whatever languages in the world. And yeah, this kind of thing happens, every so often.

So these are completely unrelated languages. Mbābaran, dog can be related to a, what is that? A proto-Pamanyungen, form gudaga, which changed in some related languages to, to other sounds. So you have gudaga and yidin and you have gudah and djerbal and, and Mbābaran, you know, they got rid of the first syllable and they got rid of the last vowel and before you knew it, you had dog.

English also has a word dog, it's not from Australia. So we have it originally referred to another kind, a particular kind of dog, a mastiff. And it's sort of taken over as our word for dog. Our older word for dog was hound, which we still sort of have. And this is the kind of thing that happens. So, you know, Persian and English have the same word for bad.

Malay and Greek have very similar looking words for eye. And it's just a coincidence. How do you know whether you're looking at a coincidence? Well you ask yourself whether you can posit regular sound changes. So you know, English and kakchigal mayan have words for mess that sound more or less the same. That's the kind of thing, you know, if I just tell you in isolation I have two languages that have the same word for mess.

If you want to know are those languages related, well the way you find out is by trying to find out whether you can come up with sound changes that apply to more than one word. You know, is it generally the case that where this language has an M, this other language has an M, where this language has an S, this other language has an S. And in the case of English in kakchigal, the answer is no, you know.

So here are a bunch of other words with M's in them. They don't have M's in kakchigal. You can't rely on it being the case that every English word with an M in it would have an M in kakchigal, even if the languages were related. But you were entitled to hope that it'll happen more than once, you know, so, so this looks like a, like a coincidence.

Yeah. Any questions about any of this? So just as an in class exercise, I wanted to try doing some historical linguistics, reconstructing some sound changes. Here are some Polynesian languages. So there are languages from the Polynesian branch of the very large Austronesian language family. Hawaiian, which is spoken in Hawaii, and Maori, which is from New Zealand, and, Tongan, which is from Tonga, and, Samoan, which is from Samoa.

These are all, languages spoken in islands of the Pacific, particularly the Eastern Pacific, or not too far from Hawaii, sort of between Hawaii and New Zealand. And these are all words that look pretty related to each other. And so let's see if we can figure out any sound correspondences. Maybe we can start by looking at the things that I have made green here.

What do you think? What sort of proto sound should we posit for the sound that is in green in these kinds of examples? There are at least two theories that we could take seriously, hypotheses we could take seriously, right? Third kind of hypothesis. Maybe, maybe it's an M, right? Yeah. But let's, let's put that possibility aside for a second. We're not going to do that unless we're driven to it.

So the two theories that we could take seriously are, you know, maybe it's a K or maybe it's a T, right? Anybody have a theory about what we ought to do? Joseph? Could I guess that maybe, majority rules are a T? Yes.

So we could do this democratically and say, yeah, you know, there are more T's on this slide than there are K's. Yeah. That's, majority, majority rules. Yeah? All of these, I mean, these are all islands, amazing story. These people, you know, started, the proto-Austronesians are supposed to have started in Taiwan and, start, you know, headed out in outrigger canoes and settled all of these islands in the Pacific, tossing continents overboard when they got too, too heavy as far as I can tell.

But you're right. Hawaiian was near the end of their journey, right? So they're supposed to have started in, in Taiwan. New Zealand and Tonga and Samoa were all closer to where they started than, than Hawaii. So maybe Hawaii is, it's, we're, we're justified to think that Hawaiian might have undergone some of the, some of the special sound changes, you know? I can also tell you something special about Hawaiian.

I don't know whether this will come up later. Hawaiian does not have a T. It's one of the few languages in the world that does not. Which is why, if you've ever heard, there's a song that is sometimes played around Christmas, which involves the Hawaiian version of Merry Christmas, Amele Kalikimaka. Mele Kalikimaka is the, the, the Hawaiian version of Merry Christmas because Hawaiian has no T and it also has no S. So they need to replace the S in Christmas with something and what they replace it with is K.
Because that's the best they can do.

That's, the closest they've got to that. So yes, Hawaiian has no T. So the idea that maybe there was a T and in Hawaiian it became a K, has some, some initial plausibility. So yeah, we'll posit a sound change in Hawaiian from T to K. And we'll posit a, a proto, some proto-Polynesian forms, that will have T in them. That is, they'll look like the Maori, Tongan, and Samoan forms. And then we'll posit the sound change in Hawaiian that changes that T into a K. Okay, so far so good.
Here are some more forms. What should we do with these? So it's either a K or it's a glottal stop.

Maybe for now we should just, sit on this problem because here, you know, Joseph's principle of, you know, doing this kind of thing democratically, maybe it doesn't help us. We've got two votes for K and two votes for glottal stop. Yeah. This might be a place where it's easier to imagine a K becoming a glottal stop than it is to imagine a glottal stop becoming a K. K's becoming glottal stops. That's a thing that happens pretty commonly in various languages. But, and in fact, that's what people standardly do in this case.

So in Hawaiian, we, we posited a K changing to a glottal stop in these kinds of examples. Maori and, Tongan are the ones that are being conservative here. We'll see an additional reason to say that in just a second, I think. Here's the additional reason to say that. Here are a bunch of more words where Tongan has glottal stops in sort of unpredictable places, right? So at the beginning of the word for day and twice in the word for love and in the middle of the words for leg and voice.
Yeah. And it's not because so if you want to learn to speak Tongan, one of the things you have to learn is that some words begin.

With glottal stops and others begin with vowels. So dawn starts with a vowel and day starts with a glottal stop. So distinguishing these from each other's, is tricky. The fact that Tongan is putting these glottal stops in places that can't be predicted, bless you, leads us to think that what's going on is that those were glottal stops in Proto-Polynesian and they've been lost in all the other languages. So here's the thing where place where, predictability trumps democracy. We can't predict where Tongan will have these glottal stops.

We can predict where Hawaiian, Maori, and Samoan will have glottal stops. So, Hawaiian and Samoan will have glottal stops wherever there's a K in Maori and Tongan, and, Maori just won't have glottal stops. Yeah. So, it's always predictable where the glottal stops are in the other languages. It's not predictable where they are in Tongan, so we'll posit them for the Proto language. So Aloha in Hawaiian, which literally means love, in Proto-Polynesian is Aloofa with two, two, glottal stops in it. And you can see incidentally another sound change that F became H, in Hawaiian and, and also in Maori.
So Hawaiian So Hawaiian has undergone sound changes, K becoming glottal stop, T becoming K and.

Glottal stop, vanishing. This is sometimes, so I'll just do that again dramatically with a, sagittal section here. So, Proto-Polynesian Ata, Don has become Aka, in Hawaiian. T has become K with the consequence that Hawaiian has no, has no T. K has become glottal stop. So, Proto-Polynesian Kula, which meant red has become Ula in Hawaiian and Proto-Polynesian, glottal stop has, has vanished. There's sometimes called a chain shift. And, you know, there are different ways of thinking about chain shifts, but they're, they're not cross-linguistically not uncommon. I'll show you another kind of example of one in just a second where you have, sound A becoming sound B, sound B becoming sound C and.

Sound C becoming sound D. And it's often difficult to determine sort of the order in which things like this happen. You know, one way to think about this is, the Hawaiians got rid of Proto-Polynesian glottal stop, and then they were like, now that we have no glottal stop, we're free to pronounce our K as glottally as we like. You know, we don't have to worry about the K getting confused with a glottal stop. And so the K began drifting backwards and becoming a glottal stop. And then they were like, wait, wait, we have no K.

Anymore. Our K is gone. It has become a glottal stop. We miss K. We will change our T into a K. And then, you know, who knows what would happen, how they would manage to come up with a T. Maybe if we keep an eye on Hawaiian, we'll get to see what they do. Yeah. It's at least one way to talk about, these kinds of chain shifts.

Another famous chain shift which is called the Great English Vowel Shift. I'm sorry, the English Great Vowel Shift. It is the reason that if you study almost any other language of Europe, you'll learn vowels that have a certain set of, sounds associated with them. They're the sounds more or less that those symbols have in the IPA. So in lots of languages this symbol is used for a vowel that's pronounced E, but in English it's pronounced I, right.

Or that in lots of languages this symbol is used for a vowel that's pronounced A, but in English, it's pronounced E. This is all because of the Great English Vowel Shift. What was the Great English Vowel Shift? It went like this. Around the 14th century, English had long vowels in these positions. English also had short vowels. We'll put those aside. So there were long vowels, those vowels that were held for a long time. And they were in those positions in the, in the mouth. So you had an E, an A, an A, and then an A, A, A, O, and O.
We had all those long vowels. And then the E and the O became diphthongs.

Sort of, if you think about holding an E for a long time, your tongue is in a position of sort of maximum tension. It's front and high. And you're supposed to hold it for a long time. And you can understand how something like that might have changed into a diphthong. So instead of holding an E for a long time, you were allowing yourself to begin the vowel with your tongue in some more central position and then just sort of gesture in the direction of an E.
So you went from E to E to I to I. And so E and O became I and O. Yeah.

And then suddenly English was like, wait, didn't we have an E and an O? Our E and O are gone. All we have is I and O. And so E and O changed. So E became E and O became U. Sort of filling the gap that, the, the first part of the, the vowel shift, left. And then long A and O both became A, right? That's why, long A in a word like fate is pronounced A. It's because, well, the A had, was off becoming an E.
So now we had a space, where there was an A and so our A became an A and our O became an O.

So all of our vowels have sort of, undergone this dance. And, if you look at, modern English dialects, you can see, a lot of these kinds of sound changes continuing today. So, you know, I come from a place where those diphthongs are getting flattened, where, where, I has a tendency to become ah, right? Or, and, and so, yeah, the, the, the vowels are continuing to chase each other around in the vowel space. I think I talked about this when we were talking about phonetic inventories that, there's work on, the kinds of vowel inventories that we find.
So, you know, there are plenty of languages.

Out there that have three vowels. And the three vowels that you have, if you have three vowels, are e, oo, and ah, right? That is, there's a front high vowel, there's a back high vowel, and there's a low vowel. That is, you have three vowels that are kind of as far apart from each other as possible. There aren't languages out there that have three vowels, and the three vowels are, you know, e, e, and eh. You know, that's, that's not a vowel system. One of my colleagues, Edward Fleming, has done a lot of work on different kinds of vowel systems and reducing them to this idea that you're trying to keep your vowels sort of maximally dispersed.

From each other, in the vocal tract. So, and that kind of thinking can be one of the ways to think about why, English underwent this vowel shift. It's like, first the high vowels changed into diphthongs, and then you have this sort of unclaimed real estate at the top of the vocal tract, right? So, once you no longer have an e or an oo, once they've changed to I and ah, there's this desire for dispersion that's pushing you to get your, some, some vowels into that space.
So, that first change kind of triggers a second change. If you study languages, one of the things that you will sometimes be told is, you know, here's a regular rule for how you form, say, different forms.

Of a noun, singulars and plurals, or different case forms, or whatever, and then here are some irregular forms. And, sometimes the irregular forms are the consequence of sound changes. So, for example, when I was briefly attempting to learn Latin in high school, we were taught. There are, certain Latin nouns, so-called third declension nouns, for which you need to memorize both the nominative singular form and, another form. We were taught to memorize the genitive singular form. Because you can't predict from the, nominative singular what the other forms will be.
So you get words like rex, which means king, and nox, which means night, and vox, which means voice. But the stems to which you add any other.

Suffix, are sort of unpredictable from the nominatives. So king, rex, the genitive and the accusative and all the other forms are formed off of a stem reg, or for night it's noct, and for voice it's vox, yeah. Anybody else here study Latin? This is all stuff that you have to learn if you're trying to learn Latin. And what I was taught at least was to think of king, the word for king as rex regis, like we were supposed to just sort of cite both of those.
So my old high school Latin teacher, Mrs. McNair, that's what she taught us. Mrs.

McNair could have taught us, she probably knew this, but she, she didn't, she didn't reveal it to us, that although you can't predict the genitive from the nominative, you can predict the nominative from the genitive. So that what's really happening in Latin is that you've got these stems which are reg, noct, and woc, and that you're forming the nominative by adding an s, and then doing some sound changes. So, you know, you take reg and you add an s, that by itself would give you regs, and then, you know, Latin doesn't allow a sequence of a g followed by an s, you make them both voiceless.
So you change the g so that it's a k, and Latin.

Spells the sequence ks, with an x. Or, you know, nocts, yeah, that would end in two stops followed by an s, k, a t, a k sound which is spelled in Latin with a c, and, and a t, and then an s, and, you know, you simplify that by getting rid of, of the t in between. Or wocs, which is, well, basically just wocs, just adding s to the, to the stem. So, Mrs. McNair could have made my life easier, if she had been willing to tell me about that.
So there have to be sound changes that, if you're willing to think about the third declension nouns this way, excuse me, you can think of them.

As, just having a nominative singular form s, and some sound changes that, obscure the fact that the nominative singular is really not so, not so complicated. She would have had to teach us a bunch of sound changes, but, these are some of them. Or, here's another example of this kind. We had a problem set earlier on Inupiaq, which is one of a number of languages that are all related to each other. They're all members of what is still, for some reason, called the Eskimo family of languages, where I say still because the word Eskimo is, not well regarded among the people to whom it refers.
It's.

It's a name for them that was come up with by the Cree. There are debates about what it means, but it sort of doesn't matter what it means. They don't like being called Eskimos, so it's unfortunate that the linguistics continues to refer to their language family as the Proto-Eskimo family. Here are some words in this language family. These are proto-forms and so I should really have stars on them. So igloo, which is a word you may have heard, it's the word for house. So all of us live in igloos, grew up in igloos, as far as this language is concerned, or Tuma or Tafsi, here's.

Some words. And there's a very general, plural suffix T, which gets added. This, the Proto-Language for this language family had, let's see now, four vowels. So there was A, Y, and U, and there's also schwa. You can see there's a schwa at the end of the for footprint. Yeah. And then, in, in the, in the history between the Proto-Language and modern Inupiaq, two sound changes took place. First, T, like the T of the plural, but not just the T of the plural, it was a general sound change.
Whenever you had a T that followed the vowel E, the T, underwent what's called palatalization, cross-linguistically.

Very common sound change, where a T becomes a CH, around a front vowel. We had something like this happen in English several times. It's why our word for cheese starts with a CH sound, comes from Latin Cassius, which started with a K, K sound. So T became CH after, after the vowel E. That's what you're seeing at the end of the word for belts. Yeah. Regular sound change. And then another sound change, Inupiaq, has a three vowel system. It only has A, E, and U. They got rid of schwa.
And in particular, they changed schwa to an E. But these sound changes took place in this order, with the.

Consequence that if you want to learn Inupiaq now, what you're taught is, well, the plural suffix is a T except after some nouns, but not all nouns that have an E at the end of them, where the T becomes a CH. And you just have to learn which, which nouns that end in E, change the T to a CH and which nouns that end in E. The vowel that's written with the letter I, don't do that. So, belt, you just have to memorize.

That's the kind of E final noun where the, the plural suffix is a CH footprint. That's the kind of E final noun where the, the, the plural suffix is still a T. This situation, which is of course distressing to people who want to learn Inupiaq, is the consequence of these sound changes occurring in this order and creating this kind of opacity. Yeah. So, what's really going on is, well, there used to be another vowel.

And, we got rid of that vowel, but, we were already committed to having CH in certain places and not others. I should say there's now dialect variation within Inupiaq. There are dialects of Inupiaq that said, no, look, this is silly. We're going to change the CH in belts, to a T. So they still have T becoming CH in some other places, but specifically the plural has gotten regularized so that it's a T everywhere. So, consequence of a history of sound changes is sometimes opacity. You have, it's kind of like the Laerdale example that I showed you before.
You have a change from A to B and you have another change.

From C to D. And the consequence of the change from C to D would create new environments for A to B, but A to B has already happened. It doesn't happen again. So as you are learning languages, if you encounter facts like this, entertain the possibility that what's going on is opacity because of sound change, occurring in a particular order. I don't know whether you'll consider whether, you'll have, whether you'll get any comfort from that thought, or whether it'll just annoy you, but, that's, sometimes the consequence.

Yeah, Laerdale, we just did Laerdale. Oh, yeah, or here's another example of the same kind of thing. Passamaquoddy, is an Algonquian language spoken up in Maine. It underwent a sound change that deleted odd numbered short vowels in words. Depending on the consonants around them, it's called, syncope. So, if you started with a word that meant, I hook a fish, that would have been something like, and what you did was to get rid of the, first and third vowels in that word. So in modern Passamaquoddy, that's now, so you've gotten rid of the first vowel and the third vowel in the word.
The.

If you get rid of that prefix, like if you wanted to say he or she hooks a fish, well, then you don't have that prefix and that changes the count. There's a vowel between.

The P and the T in the I form, but not in the he or she form. There's a vowel between the T and the H in the he or she form, but not in the I form. If you look at the online Passamaquoddy dictionary, you see lots of examples of this kind of thing. And it's sometimes radically different, as a consequence of this sound change.
So for example, here's another, another instance of the same thing. I'm sorry about it. It was originally.

Getting rid of the first and third vowels in that, would have given you, which is the, the modern way to say I'm sorry about it. If you don't add the prefix, if you want to say he or she is sorry, you're going to get rid of the first and third vowels. If nothing else happened, that would give you, but, in fact, M next to S tends to become a P. So, you know, again, the, the rule is, get rid of the odd numbered short vowels in the sequence. And that's the kind of rule that makes sense if you think, yeah, it had a stress system that, stressed the even numbered vowels, right. So, I hook a fish was, you know,, and then they got rid of the first and the third vowels because they weren't stressed.

It's getting rid of them because they're not stressed. And Passamaquoddy does have relatives that have sound, have, stress systems like that where you stress the, the even numbered vowels.
But that is not Passamaquoddy stress system anymore. So.

Passamaquoddy underwent all these sound changes because of the stress system that they used to have, that they used to share with some of their relatives. And then they changed their stress system. Their modern stress system goes, stress the first vowel and stress every other vowel counting backwards from the end. So, you get words like, which is he or she talks like that, where you're stressing the first vowel and the second vowel. First vowel because you always stress the first vowel and the second vowel because it's two vowels, back from the end.

So stress is on the first syllable and it's on the even numbered syllables counting backwards from the end of the word. So they've, they've innovated this new stress system. I want to end today because I think we have enough time for me to, to heap scorn on, on a couple of things. I want to end today with two bad ideas. So one of the reasons that I'm talking to you about historical linguistics, there are several.

One is that it's fun. It's kind of interesting to try to trace back these sound changes. But another is that historical linguistics is the kind of thing that is sometimes done badly. So you'll see in popular press people saying things like, Hey, this language is related to that language or this language has borrowed this word from that language. And if, if you have any skepticism about that, what you want them to do is show you regular sound correspondences or regular rules for how words are related to other words.
So there was a popular book for a while, that proposed that the, the native American languages, were all descended from Chinese that the, the Mandarin Chinese had, had encountered.

North America first and that their language had, taken over North America and that all of the modern languages, were descended from Chinese. And the the examples were things like, you know, in modern Mandarin, there is a greeting, ni hao. And Hey, if you watch Hollywood movies, you will hear native Americans greeting each other with how, and there, you know, so these, the, clearly these are related to each other. How actually is a greeting in Lakota. It's a, a greeting that, Lakota men use to greet other men.
It's not related to the Mandarin expression ni hao. And the reason I can say that with so much confidence is that.

Well, no one has ever found sound correspondences or anything like that relating anything else in Lakota to, anything else in, in Chinese. There are slightly less silly claims that people have made using what's sometimes called megalocomparison. So let's skip glottocrinology. Oh, so glottocrinology, I'll leave this on the slide, but glottocrinology is an attempt to sort of carbon date language splits. So the idea is you take a list of basic basic vocabulary. You figure out how many cognates the two languages share on the list.

And you start with the assumption that cognate loss happens at a constant rate. This is the figure that has been offered by glottocrinologists. And then you do some math. And this is something that you see people seriously saying. So if you look in, historical linguistics, usually not done by linguists. It's often being done by biologists. They'll say, we know that these languages are related to the time depth of X. And what they mean is we've done glottocrinology, which starts from the assumption that you can do this kind of carbon dating of, languages, you know, by looking at cognate lists and figuring out the rate at which two languages cease to have cognates with each other.
The problem is that the base assumption that cognate loss.

Happens at a constant rate is false. And, there's no one has figured out a way around that. There doesn't seem to be any way to correct for it. So if you see people using glottocrinology, you should treat them with skepticism. The other, the other bad idea that's in here is megalocomparison. This is attempts to reconstruct proto-world, which were very sort of in vogue at a certain time. People thought if we do lots and lots of this cross-linguistic comparison, we'll be able to figure out the ancestor language for all of the languages in the world.

The work, has never involved discovering general sound changes that relate languages to each other. It always involves going back to saying, Hey, look, that word in that language looks kind of like that word in that language. I bet they're related. That's, that's how that work is always gone. So those are two ideas, bad ideas that you should be skeptical about. This has been my public service announcement for today. Are there any questions about either of those bad ideas.

I'll put more detail about the bad ideas in the slides. Okay, cool. Go forth and, I will see you next week. I'll see you next week. I'll see you next week. Bye. Bye.

Many of them died along the way. So not all of them went, I guess, which is why there are Cherokee speakers in two different areas.

People have a powerful motivation to sort of want to say that they can speak the language even if they can't. For a lot of these languages, there are also forces in the other direction. So a history of people being punished traditionally, being taken to residential schools and that kind of thing, in attempts to stop them from speaking their indigenous languages have led people to want to lie in the other direction, say that they don't speak a language, when in fact they do.
